#Hiring Associate III - BPM
EXP: 5-7 yrs
Job Description

Contract : 6 Month
Job Title: Senior Claims Adjuster, US Healthcare (Payer)
Shift: 5:30 PM - 2:30 AM IST (US Shift)
Experience: 5+ Years (Payer-Side Mandatory)
Role Summary
We are looking for a highly skilled Senior Claims Adjuster with deep expertise in payer-side claims adjudication, including complex claims adjustments, to support high-impact US healthcare operations. The role demands strong analytical capability, system knowledge, and the ability to interpret payer guidelines with precision in a Healthcare environment.
Key Responsibilities
Perform end-to-end claims adjudication and adjustments for professional and institutional claims
Handle complex claims adjustments, including:
Multi-line claims
Pricing/reimbursement corrections
Contract/fee schedule interpretation
Coordination of Benefits (COB) scenarios
Review and validate eligibility, benefits, authorizations, and medical necessity (non-clinical)
Investigate and resolve denials, appeals, reprocessing, and exception cases
Ensure accurate application of payer policies, business rules, and reimbursement methodologies
Work on manual adjudication scenarios where auto-processing fails
Ensure compliance with HIPAA, client SLAs, and quality benchmarks
Support RCA, audits, and continuous improvement initiatives
Act as an SME/mentor for junior team members
Required Skills & Expertise
Strong experience in payer-side claims adjudication and adjustments
Proven ability to handle complex claims scenarios independently
Deep knowledge of:
Claims lifecycle
EOB interpretation
Pricing logic & reimbursement methodologies
Expertise in CPT, ICD-10, HCPCS coding systems
Hands-on experience with payer platforms; HealthRules knowledge preferred
Strong analytical, problem-solving, and decision-making skills
Ability to manage high-volume workloads with high accuracy
Effective communication skills (client & internal stakeholders)
Eligibility Criteria
Minimum 5+ years of experience in US Healthcare payer claims adjudication
Mandatory experience in BPO / KPO environment
Graduate
Certifications such as Certified Professional Coder (CPC) are an added advantage
Preferred Qualifications
Experience in complex/high-dollar claims handling
Exposure to automation / AI-assisted claims adjudication tools
Experience in quality audits / SME / team handling roles
Knowledge of Medicare, Medicaid, and Commercial plans
As part of UST, candidates are expected to demonstrate:
Humility: Respectful collaboration and openness to feedback
Humanity: Empathy towards members, providers, and colleagues
Integrity: Ethical processing and strict adherence to compliance
Customer Focus: Delivering high-quality outcomes aligned with client expectations
Excellence: Continuous improvement and ownership of results

#hiring Director of Legal

Apply here: https://lnkd.in/ghBupCwN

About us Foxglove is a non-profit that exists to make the use of technology fair for all. When Big Tech companies abuse their power, their workers or the planet and when governments use technology to oppress, exclude or discriminate we litigate and campaign to fix it.  Big Tech companies have become so large gobbling up a huge slice of the global marketplace and an unprecedented treasure hoard of user data that they re now more powerful than many states. The harmful effects of this concentration of power are everywhere threats to our democracy, to our privacy, decimated workers rights and platforms rife with disinformation and hate. Big Tech and AI data centres are rapidly expanding, resulting in huge strain on energy and water supplies. Worldwide governments are ploughing ahead with the use of algorithms and mass data systems to cut costs and increase efficiency often resulting in digital tools that entrench unfairness and leave the most vulnerable in society in crisis. All these problems are only getting worse with generative AI. Foxglove works to bring the rule of law to the tech and AI giants who have upended our public square, workplaces, and social lives. We have a strong track record. We ve launched landmark cases seeking structural changes to big tech s harmful business models, supported 180+ Facebook content moderators fired for trying to form a union to sue Facebook and their outsourcing company, Sama winning world-first judgements. Were urging competition regulators worldwide to stop Google s theft of independent news. We ve filed the UK s first legal challenge to a data centre permission decision, forced disclosure of secret contracts between tech giants and the NHS, stopped a racist Home Office visa streaming algorithm, helped make grading fair for UK A-level students and challenged the Department of Work and Pension s use of an algorithm unfairly flagging disabled people for benefit fraud investigations.  We are a small but growing team of lawyers, communications experts, and campaigners. We are a CIC, not a practising law firm. We partner with legal firms on cases, directing litigation in multiple jurisdictions. Our work is global, and we work in partnership with lawyers, civil society, unions, and people impacted by Big Tech. About you You are a highly experienced lawyer with a strong interest in using the law to hold governments and companies to account. You see the law as a tool for structural change, not just individual wins. You think strategically about how litigation, campaigning, and coalition-building can work together. You appreciate the value and impact of movements and are as comfortable drafting legal arguments as you are speaking to a journalist, or rallying those whove never heard of judicial review to a joint cause. You care about power, meaning who has it, who doesnt, and how to shift it. You follow the ways Big Tech

Most 10–100 person SMEs assume "AI governance" means buying a big GRC platform or hiring a compliance manager.

For most UK firms running on Xero, Microsoft 365, HubSpot or Shopify, it usually means fixing the plumbing, not writing more policy.

The practical move is to add an AI governance layer on top of tools already in place over the next 90 days.

Here is one way to do it.

Step 1: Decide what must be provable.
Write 5–10 sentences that start with: "If were audited, we would need to show…" or "If a client disputes X, we need to prove…".
Examples: who approved spends over £2,000, which version of a proposal was signed, that complaints were answered within the SLA.
These become audit trail requirements.

Step 2: Capture events from existing systems, not people.
Hook into Xero approvals, SharePoint / OneDrive "Signed" folders, ticket closures in Zendesk or Intercom, key emails in 365.
Each time one of those events fires, create a log entry with who, what, when, plus the raw document or message.
No new forms and no extra boxes to tick.

Step 3: Let AI enrich and classify.
Use off the shelf models to extract contract terms, classify emails as complaints or risk, and summarise reasons for approvals in plain English.
Turn messy activity into structured fields that can be searched, filtered and compared.

Step 4: Centralise logs where nobody can quietly edit history.
For most SMEs this is a SharePoint list, SQL table or well locked Google Sheet.
If something changes, add a new row that references the previous one and do not overwrite.

Step 5: Pilot 1–2 high risk workflows first.
Supplier spend over £50k a month, contracts signed without legal review, refunds and discounts, repeated complaints.
Run AI checks in shadow mode for 2–4 weeks, then tighten rules once the signal is trusted.

Done right, this adds less than 10 seconds to each action and gives a governance layer HMRC, banks and clients actually respect, without rebuilding the stack or hiring a compliance team.

The full design, tooling options and rollout plan are here: https://lnkd.in/e5Ukwhbm

You don’t need to be on stage at Demo Day to learn from it.

If I were preparing founders for a high-stakes pitch \(accelerator, VC, or internal investment committee\), I’d build around a simple 6-slide spine:

1\) Problem
Define 1 painful, expensive, specific problem. Bonus if you can quantify it for your market.

2\) Why Now
Show the shift: regulation, technology \(AI/BIM\), cost, or behavior that makes this moment different.

3\) Solution
1 visual, 3–4 bullets. Show how it integrates with today’s workflows, not a fantasy stack.

4\) Traction
Use the “traction triangle”:
- Growth rate: MoM/YoY revenue, users, projects, or pilots.
- Retention/usage: cohort retention, weekly active teams, activation to “first value”.
- Pipeline quality: qualified leads, conversion rates, sales cycle length, win rate.

5\) Business Model
Who pays, how much, how often. Mention typical contract size and payback period.

6\) Ask
Capital, introductions, pilot sites, or strategic partners. Be precise on amount, timeline, and use of funds.

For live Q&A, I expect investors to ask:
1\) How big is the market? → Size it with TAM/SAM/SOM and # of ideal customers.
2\) Why are customers buying now? → Reference adoption curves, budget shifts, or regulation.
3\) What proof do you have that users stay? → Retention %, churn, and usage frequency.
4\) What does a “good” customer look like? → Segment, ACV, payback, reference clients.
5\) How do you acquire customers today? → Channels, CAC range, and conversion rates.
6\) How long does a deal take to close? → Average sales cycle and win rate.
7\) Who are your main competitors? → Short list, and your measurable edge \(cost, speed, accuracy\).
8\) How will you use this round? → % to product, GTM, hiring, runway months.
9\) What are the biggest risks? → Top 2–3, with mitigation steps and leading indicators.
10\) What does success look like in 18–24 months? → Revenue, user, and key milestone targets.

Rehearsal method I like: 3 takes in 20–30 minutes.
- Take 1 – Clarity \(10 min\): Talk through the 6 slides slowly. Fix jargon and long sentences.
- Take 2 – Speed \(5–7 min\): Deliver inside the Demo Day time limit. Cut anything non-essential.
- Take 3 – Investor-grade specificity \(5–7 min\): Add numbers to every claim you can quantify.

What’s the hardest slide for you—traction, market sizing, or differentiation?

If you’re preparing for a Demo Day or investor review and want a simple checklist, DM me and I’ll share a version you can adapt.

Asana and Canva rode the same AI wave but are seeing completely opposite outcomes. This is not a coincidence. It's a signal.

a16z just released data on which SaaS companies are winning and losing among large AI spenders. (Mar '26)

The winners: 📍 Canva: +101% 📍 Replit: +78% 📍 Vercel: +72% 📍 HubSpot: +63%

The losers: 📍 Asana: (45%) 📍 Twilio: (36%) 📍 Atlassian: (21%) 📍 Adobe: (15%)
→ Look at the pattern carefully.

Winners are infrastructure, creation, and deployment tools. Losers are coordination, workflow management, and convenience layers.

An AI agent does not need a project management UI to track tasks or a notification tool to send an alert. 
All it needs is
a database
a deployment pipeline
a creative canvas and a CRM with real data in it.

→ The software that survives is built on hard problems.
Agents automate workflows on top of those systems. They cannot replace the foundation underneath.

I think about this every time I look at what roles companies are actually hiring for.

The demand is not for people who know how to use software. The demand is for people who can build what agents run on like:
Cloud engineers.
Data engineers.
AI deployment specialists.

The people who lay the track, not the people who manage the train schedule.
→ Pricing will shift, too.

If an agent accesses your CRM for 2 secs to update a lead, no enterprise is paying full seat price for that. Software that doesn't adapt to per-task, per-agent billing will lose the contract.

The AI era is not killing software spend. 
It is making software earn its place.

Which side of this table is your skill set on?

"India sounds great in theory. But what happens if it goes wrong?"

That's the question every board asks when a founder pitches India hiring for the first time.

Here's how to answer it, objection by objection:

"What's the compliance exposure?"
$99/month puts a legal employer between your US company and every Indian labour law, payroll filing, and statutory obligation. 
Your board's liability doesn't change. Your exposure does.

"How long before we see ROI?"
First hire live within 8 working hours. 
60–70% salary savings from month one. 
For a 120-person B2B SaaS company hiring 4 engineers in Hyderabad, payback on EOR fees is measured in weeks, not quarters.

"What if India doesn't work out?"
No entity to wind down. 
No 6–12 month liquidation process. 
You're testing the market, not betting the company on it. 
EOR entry is reversible. An entity isn't an easy walk.

"Is this just outsourcing?"
Cost is a benefit, not the reason. Our clients expand to India to access specialised skills in AI/ML, data engineering, and cybersecurity, roles that are 6–9 month searches in the US right now. 

The process the board sees on paper: 
Offer → Contract → Payroll → Employee Active.

Simple. Auditable. Defensible.

We blame AI and companies for layoffs. But crude oil is the real culprit. Here’s how-: 

Crude Oil is Rising.  
But the real question is — who pays the price?

Not just the government.  
Not just companies.  
👉 Jobs do.

When crude prices go up, India feels it immediately. Why?

Because we import most of our oil.

And that triggers a chain reaction:

⛽ Fuel costs rise →  
🏭 Companies’ expenses increase →  
📉 Margins shrink →  
🚫 Hiring slows down  

It doesn’t stop there…

🚚 Transport & logistics cut operations  
✈️ Aviation goes into cost-control mode  
🏢 MSMEs — the biggest job creators — start struggling  

Then comes the silent killer: Inflation

💸 Higher fuel prices → Higher prices everywhere  
🛒 Consumers spend less → Demand drops  
📉 Businesses earn less → Fewer jobs created  

Short term reality?  
Hiring freezes. Contract jobs vanish. Growth pauses.

---

But here’s the uncomfortable truth:

We talk about GDP.  
We celebrate stock market highs.  

👉 But ignore how something as basic as crude oil  
can quietly decide who gets hired… and who doesn’t.

---

Question:  
Do you think India is too vulnerable to global oil shocks?

Almost every freelance contract has a clause in it that says if my work isn't satisfactory or doesn't meet the appropriate professional standards, then either I have to redo it at no extra cost until it is good enough, or the client can refuse to accept it and not pay.

What if AI had the same terms and conditions? If it doesn't do what you told it to do, you don't pay. 

(Just thinking - all those anecdotal reports of rogue AIs wiping out databases, code repositories, etc... the companies effectively paid them to do that. And then, most likely, paid them to rebuild the mess they made. It's like hiring someone to help you move some furniture, but instead they smash all your windows, rip up your carpets, and set fire to your office - then charge you for the time it took to do it and charge you for the repairs.)

•  🚨 The Immigration Tax: The silent, exhausting pressure of proving your worth in UK tech while navigating unwritten cultural rules and visa dynamics.
•  🎭 The Weaponisation of 'Cultural Fit': How HR terminology is actively used as a gatekeeper to keep diverse, brilliant minds out of the room.

Multi-Agent Systems: When One Agent Isn't Enough

Some problems are too complex for a single agent.

A supply chain disruption requires agents monitoring inventory, weather, freight routes, and supplier contracts — simultaneously.

A clinical workflow requires agents handling scheduling, documentation, lab results, and patient communication — in coordination.

Multi-Agent Systems are networks of specialized agents that collaborate, delegate, and self-correct — orchestrated by a central intelligence layer.

Think of it as hiring a specialist team where every member is AI-native, always available, and never siloed.

If your AI screening tool systematically penalises CVs with employment gaps, you may be indirectly discriminating against women returning from maternity leave, people with long-term disabilities, and specific ethnic demographics. That's the warning the ICO effectively made in March 2026.

Here's what quietly changed in the legal backdrop in 2026, and why HR teams need to pay attention:

• The Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 came fully into force on 5 February 2026. It replaced Article 22's blanket prohibition on automated decision-making with a legitimate-interests framework.

• The ICO's updated AI and Biometrics Strategy explicitly states they'll scrutinise the use of automated decision-making in recruitment in 2026, looking specifically at transparency, discrimination and redress.

• The ICO consultation on draft ADM guidance runs until 29 May 2026, with updated recruitment and selection guidance landing later this year.

• The Equality Act 2010 remains the sharpest risk. AI doesn't get a pass — if a tool produces a discriminatory outcome, the employer is liable.

The trap most SMBs walk into isn't malicious. It's this: you buy an AI screening tool, it works "well enough", and six months later you've made 80 hiring decisions with no documented human review, no Data Protection Impact Assessment, and no way to explain to a candidate why they were screened out.

A seven-point audit to run before the end of this quarter:

1. Map every AI touchpoint in your hiring funnel — including CV screening, video interview analysis, and chatbots
2. Run a DPIA before you deploy, not after an ICO letter arrives
3. Ask your vendor whose CVs the model was trained on
4. Define what "meaningful human review" actually looks like in your process
5. Write candidate-facing transparency notices
6. Test for adverse impact across protected characteristics
7. Set a retraining and monitoring cadence — at least quarterly

AI in recruitment is lawful in the UK. Careless AI in recruitment isn't. The difference is mostly documentation — and whether anyone has actually read the vendor contract.

Full 7-point audit checklist: https://lnkd.in/e6qGazYU

Most AI governance guides give you theory. This one gives you a deadline.

Colorado SB 205 enforcement starts July 1, 2026.
NYC Local Law 144 is already issued 847 violation notices in 2025.
The EU AI Act has been in force since February 2026.

If your organization uses AI in hiring, lending, insurance, or healthcare and hasn't built a governance program yet, you're already behind.

Here's what a 90-day AI governance sprint actually looks like:

📋 Weeks 1–2: Discover ALL AI tools in use (including shadow AI, most orgs find 20+ unauthorized tools)
⚖️ Weeks 3–4: Get AI legal counsel. Review your top vendor contracts.
🏛️ Weeks 5–6: Form a governance council with REAL decision authority, not another committee that produces slide decks
✅ Weeks 7–8: Schedule bias audits. Begin impact assessments. Train HR.
🧪 Weeks 9–10: Run a tabletop AI incident exercise before a real one happens
📊 Weeks 11–12: Board presentation + governance calendar

The organizations that handle AI risk well aren't the ones with the most sophisticated ethics frameworks.

They're the ones that found their AI tools, classified the risk, got legal involved early, and built a decision structure with actual authority.

Total Year 1 cost for a 50–500 person org: $33K–$75K. A single NYC LL144 fine can run $1,500/day.

The math isn't complicated.

🔗 Full practical guide (with free templates, tool recommendations, and contract clauses to demand from vendors):

https://lnkd.in/dkmHt-Fr

This role is about ownership, problem-solving, and engineering judgment — not just years of experience. You’ll work on building new services, modernizing legacy systems, and ensuring production stability in a high-impact environment.


🔧 What You’ll Do:
• Work across both modern .NET and legacy .NET Framework codebases
• Understand existing systems and improve them without unnecessary rewrites
• Design and build robust APIs (REST / gRPC) with strong contract-first thinking

• Debug real production issues using logs, traces, memory dumps, and profiling tools
• Deploy and manage applications across Windows and Linux environments
• Write meaningful tests (unit + integration using xUnit/NUnit & Testcontainers)
• Document decisions clearly through PRs, design docs, and commits

• Deep understanding of API design (REST, gRPC, OpenAPI)

– Windows (IIS, Windows Services, Active Directory, PowerShell)

One year into my MBA in Supply Chain Management, and honestly nobody prepares you for how fast this field is actually moving.
I picked SCM because I wanted to understand how the world moves goods. What I didn't expect was that by the time I finish my degree, half the frameworks I'm learning today will already have evolved.
Three things I'm seeing in every case study, every guest lecture, and every industry conversation right now:

→ AI is changing what "good forecasting" even means. We studied a case where a company reduced stockouts by 30% not by hiring more planners, but by switching to AI-driven demand sensing. The lesson wasn't about the technology. It was about the mindset shift from reacting to disruptions to seeing them coming. That's the skill I want to build.

→ India is the supply chain story nobody in my class expected to study this much. China+1 isn't a geopolitical theory anymore it's showing up in real procurement decisions. PLI schemes in electronics and pharma are pulling serious global investment. As someone studying SCM in India, that hits differently. We're not just studying global supply chains we're sitting inside one that's being built.

→ Sustainability went from an elective topic to a core one fast. Scope 3 emissions, ESG supplier audits, carbon-linked contracts these were footnotes in older textbooks. Today they're showing up in actual RFPs. And I'll never forget what my professor told us: "If you can't help your company measure its supply chain emissions, you're already behind."

Honestly, the more I learn, the more I realise that the best supply chain professionals aren't just operationally sharp. They think like economists, read like analysts, and question like strategists.

A big part of why I'm able to see the industry this way is because of the mentors I've been fortunate to learn from. Suresh Yadav and Raghav Acharya thank you for pushing us beyond textbooks and making us think about supply chain not just as a function, but as a strategic lever. Your guidance has genuinely shaped how I approach this field.

Still figuring out a lot but that curiosity is what brought me here, and the right teachers are making all the difference.

Not all AI is created equal—and the EU is making that the law.
The EU AI Act has officially established a risk-based taxonomy, effectively creating a "Risk Pyramid" that dictates exactly how artificial intelligence must be governed. If your company is building, buying, or deploying AI, here is where your technology might fall:
🔺 Unacceptable Risk (Prohibited): These systems are strictly banned. This includes AI used for social scoring, deploying manipulative or deceptive techniques, and real-time remote biometric identification by law enforcement in public spaces.
🟠 High Risk (Strictly Regulated): These applications face rigorous regulatory requirements before they can hit the market. If you are using AI for hiring, credit scoring, medical devices, or essential public services, you must implement strict data governance, mandatory human oversight, and continuous risk management.
🟡 Limited Risk (Transparency Required): The primary focus here is disclosure. If you are deploying chatbots, emotion recognition, or systems that generate deepfakes, you are legally required to ensure users know they are interacting with AI.
🟢 Minimal/Low Risk (Unregulated): This tier includes the vast majority of AI systems currently in use, such as spam filters or AI-enabled video games. These face no mandatory obligations under the Act, though voluntary codes of conduct are encouraged.
Navigating these complex, shifting compliance tiers across your third-party vendor agreements is exactly why we built Kartez Contract—giving your legal team the automated guardrails to innovate safely without drowning in manual review.
Where does your company's AI fall on the pyramid? Let me know in the comments below. 👇

The fear surrounding governance and AI is a common source of questions I get asked. 

The question that actually matters is simpler: do you trust it enough to deploy it, and have you defined what happens when it gets something wrong?

It's the same way you think about hiring someone, you set guardrails, you decide what they're ready for, and you work out who's responsible when things go sideways. Some people you'd send into any room unsupervised. Others you'd never put in front of a client until they've earned it. The judgement is yours to make as the person running the business.

Agentic AI is no different. The complexity is just in where the responsibility sits. Adopt a third-party system and that responsibility is shared, so get it written clearly into your contracts before you start. Build in house, and you own it entirely. 

Either way, "the AI did it" is not a defence, and it shouldn't feel like one.

I was asked this during the Gibraltar AI summit, where I discussed building businesses in the AI and how to decide where your investment can return the most value. Find my full keynote in the comments below.

The Death of Loyalty: Navigating Individual Allegiance in 2026

The "old psychological contract" isn't just fraying- it’s gone.
In 2026, the traditional trade-off of "tenure for security" has been replaced by a more fluid, individualistic landscape. We are seeing a massive shift where employees no longer pledge allegiance to a logo, but to a purpose and a culture that respects their unique way of thinking.

Why the old contract broke:
* The Trust Gap: Years of rapid restructuring have left talent feeling like a line item rather than a stakeholder.

* The Value Pivot: Post-2025, people are prioritizing autonomy and values-alignment over a 20-year gold watch.

* The Rise of Agency: In an AI-driven market, the best talent knows their value is portable.

The Inclusion Edge: A New Blueprint for Loyalty 
Interestingly, we are finding that "loyalty" isn't dead-it has just evolved. Look at the data surrounding neuro-inclusion: Organisations that provide genuine psychological safety and sensory-friendly environments for neurodivergent talent see retention rates upwards of 90%.

Why? Because when a workplace moves beyond "one size fits all" to accommodate different cognitive styles, they build a bridge of reciprocal trust. 

When you value how a person actually works, they value the place they work.
How Leaders Can Build New Alignment: The goal is no longer "retention at all costs"- it’s alignment by design.

1. Move from Transactional to Transformational: Focus on who your people become while they are with you.

2. Shared Values as the New Anchor: Alignment happens when the company’s "Why" mirrors the individual’s "What next."

3. Support as a Retention Tool: Loyalty in 2026 belongs to the leaders who trade "unwritten rules" for radical transparency and inclusive support.

The bridge between where we were and where we’re going is built on trust, cognitive diversity, and human-centric leadership. 

Drop us a message and see how we can get you ready for the future

Weekly Leadership Map 🗺 - #2: We identified every leadership member at Legora. Here are the findings:

👨‍⚖️ Three founders. Zero law degrees.
Max Junestrand studied Computer Science and Machine Learning at KTH, Stockholm. August Erséus came from tech and AI. Sigge Labor taught himself to code at 17 and built his first company while still a teenager. None of them came from law. And yet, their first major clients were White & Case, Linklaters, and Dentons. Understanding what AI can do with a contract didn't require a legal background.

👋 Nobody selling it came from law.
Every VP-level commercial hire in the last 18 months came from enterprise SaaS: Zoom, Vanta, a fintech, consumer tech. Legora made a deliberate call to hire people who know how to scale revenue, then teach them the legal use case. Meanwhile, the Head of Canada left to join McCarthy Tétrault, not as a lawyer, but as a legal tech operator. He built and sold a legal tech company (acquired by iManage), spent three years running it post-exit, and is now inside one of Canada's largest law firms. Interesting to see that the firms aren't just buying legal AI, but also hiring the people who built it.

🌍 Stockholm built it. New York is selling it.
The CEO relocated to New York ahead of the Series D. The CFO, CRO, and VP Revenue NA are all US-based. Engineering and product remain in Stockholm. The commercial spine is being built in the US.

🚻 Strong pipeline, thin conversion upward.
Around a third of Director and Head-of level roles are held by women. At VP and above: fewer than one in five. The three most senior appointments in early 2026 were all male. One internal promotion to VP in that same window, from within the sales team.

Which company should we map next?

Everyone can translate tech into business terms. Translating it into revenue outcomes is a different job entirely.


Over the past few years, I've had the privilege of working with and learning from some of the best "Tech-to-Revenue Translators" in the business.

And here is what I learned. 

Tech-to-business is a deck. Tech-to-revenue is a contract that survives quarterly review.

When I was running messaging partnerships at Meta, the deals that actually closed were not the ones with the best product story. 

They were the ones where pricing matched the buyer's procurement cycle, where the sales enablement copy survived a translation into a regional partner's CRM, & where someone had argued through the contract clause about data processing in a German legal entity at 11pm on a Tuesday.

None of that work shows up on a slide. 

All of it shows up on the balance sheet.

The pattern I notice talking to people running senior Tech & AI commercial hiring: Tech-to-business candidates outnumber tech-to-revenue candidates by a wide margin. 

The difference is not analytical capability. 

It is reps & the reps come from years of driving & closing deals & partnerships where no one in the room is impressed by the deck.

#Hiring

Your next job interview might not be for you.
It could be for the AI you built, earning $10k/month.

RevenueCat recently posted a new role: Agentic AI Developer Advocate. But here's the twist: they're not just looking for a human to build an agent. They're explicitly stating they will be interviewing the AI agent itself for a paid contract role offering over $10,000 per month.

This isn't a hypothetical. This agent would be responsible for creating content, running growth experiments, and providing product feedback. The criteria for success are clear, and the company is asking potential applicants: "Are you (or did you build) the right agent?" This marks a significant shift in how we think about work, talent acquisition, and the very definition of a "developer advocate." It's a real-world scenario where AI agents are being considered as direct contributors to a company's bottom line.

What does this development mean for human-centric job markets moving forward?

Most teams get this wrong.

Permanent = stability
Contract (Freelance) = speed

But in Data and AI, it comes down to one thing:

Do you need long-term capability or immediate delivery?

Permanent
- Long-term build
- Ownership matters

Contract (Freelance)
- Delivery is behind
- Need experience now

Where it breaks
Hiring permanent when you need speed or contractors without a clear scope

What I’m seeing more
- Contractors (Freelancers) to unlock delivery
- Permanent hires built after

the most expensive mistake i see founders making in 2026 is this:

they still think LinkedIn is "just another platform." 

it’s way more than that. 

i’ve spent my phd research looking at how trust is actually constructed in the digital age and the results are clear: logos don't sell; faces do.

look how the world's most successful companies are treating LinkedIn like a distribution engine.

in 2026, the "chief storyteller" is a mechanical necessity for scaling.

• investors don’t bet on companies; they bet on founders they’ve been "digitally stalking" for months.

• top-tier talent doesn't join a mission they haven't heard the founder articulate.

• enterprise buyers don't sign 6-figure contracts with "logos." they sign with authorities.

so if you still think "founder-led growth" is a trend, you aren't paying attention to the math.

look at the founders who actually move the needle here:

• Deepinder Goyal (zomato): 
he shares raw notes on culture, recruitment, and product nuance. 
when he posts a hiring call on his personal profile, it gets 100x the high-quality applications that a standard job portal ever would. 
he is the distribution engine for talent.

• Kunal Shah (cred): 
love him or hate him, he is a master of "provocative hooks." he doesn’t sell credit card bills; he sells curiosity. 
he uses his personal profile to build an intellectual monopoly. 
people join cred because they want to be part of the "system" he talks about.

• Nithin & Nikhil Kamath (zerodha): 
i notice they almost never talk about "trading." 
they talks about creator economy, health, sustainable growth, and the ethics of business, built so much institutional trust that zerodha barely spends on marketing. 

if you aren't using your profile to:

1. challenge the "default" way your industry operates.

2. share the technical nuances that ai can't replicate.

3. build a direct line to your buyers that doesn't depend on an ad budget.

...then you are just becoming a distribution bottleneck.

If you’re still hiring an outsourcing partner to simply “throw bodies” at a problem, you’re buying a horse and buggy in the age of the electric jet.

In 2026, the industry has hit a tipping point: Traditional outsourcing is dead. It has been replaced by a hybrid model where senior talent is force-multiplied by AI-driven automation. At UVIK, we see this as the new standard.

1️⃣ From "Uptime" to "Predictive Resilience"
We used to measure success by how fast we fixed a break. Now, we use AI-driven observability to predict failures before they happen. Self-healing scripts trigger automatically.
The Strategy: Don’t pay for recovery; pay for continuous, interrupted availability.

2️⃣ Killing the "Technical Debt" Tax
AI-assisted code reviews and automated refactoring ensure every line of Python is optimized as it’s written. This prevents the "spaghetti code" that typically plagues long-term offshore projects.
The Pro Tip: Demand a partner that uses AI to audit their own code quality in real-time.

3️⃣ Hyper-Speed Pipelines
CI/CD is no longer just a script; it’s intelligent. AI-enabled pipelines decide which tests are critical based on specific code changes, slashing deployment times by up to 60%.
The Bottom Line: Your competitors are deploying 10x a day while you’re stuck behind a manual QA gate.

💡 My advice:
📌 Audit the Toolchain: Ask: "Which AI-agents are integrated into your DevOps pipeline?" No answer? No contract.

📌 Efficiency over Headcount: If a vendor suggests 10 juniors for a task 2 seniors with AI could handle, walk away.

📌 Automate the Growth: The value isn't in the code; it’s in the automation logic that manages it.

If your partner isn't bringing AI to the table, they aren't bringing a future.

After 200 hours of vibe coding with Claude Code, i've built my own SaaS reporting tool.


It's been a long journey, and it's not the end, just the beginning. The last 8 weeks i've been working Monday to Sunday to ship the first version. It's crazy how much I've learnt about AI while vibe coding (more on that in future posts).

I've always been frustrated by most reporting tools, they were missing the features a SaaS startup actually needs.

The challenges I kept hitting:

1. No MRR (monthly recurring revenue) schedule linked to contracts signed and accounting. No Customers tab to keep records of contracts, invoices sent, and invoices that need to go out.
   
2. B2B SaaS forecasts in annual ARR targets and new logos, not percentages. Founders think "£3m of new ARR this year, 40 new logos to get there." Every tool I tried wanted a fixed amount or a %, and there was also no way to track targets achieved vs targets remaining linkedin to pipeline.
   
3. No proper People & Hiring tab, this one pissed me off the most. Founders want a clear list of positions to hire over the next 3 months with planned salaries, by department. Every tool gave you a single hiring row. No summary of forecasted hires, no department view, none of the small things that make it actually usable.
   
4. Startup budgets are complicated, especially for SaaS/AI. You don't just say "£10k to Sales and Marketing." You budget for specific events you might go to, travel, sales partners in different regions. 
   
So what makes this tool different?

1. It's very niched built for SaaS that deal with annual contracts, deferred income and renewals.
   
2. Customers tab as the single source of truth. Every contract, invoice and deferred income release lives against the customer record and MRR schedule rolls up from there automatically.
   
3. Invoice Schedule that auto-creates future invoices from each contract - annual upfront, monthly, quarterly, ramped, with VAT and payment lag, so the forecast knows exactly when cash lands.
   
4. Forecast built in annual ARR targets and new logos, per product, per region. The engine builds the cohorts, applies churn at renewal, flows it through P&L, BS & CF.
   
5. People & Hiring with role-by-role planning. Position, department, salary, NI, pension, start date. Clear summary of who you're hiring and when, by department.
   
6. Budget items that match how startups actually budget, events, travel, partners by region, each with its own payment terms.

and a lot more stuff and more to add. Anyway attaching a few pictures. I am testing it now and there are still some bugs to fix, will need probably another week or two to make sure everything works properly. Every day will post about what I learned about Vibe Coding with Claude Code.

I am very very impressed by the tool i built in the last 2 months!! Still testing the tool on a startup. Apologies for not having some demo data in the screenshots, will make a better presentation next time! :)

Stay tunned!

Today’s job market is broken in many ways.

1. Actual openings inside companies have reduced drastically.  Only role circulations are happening within big organisations .  
2. Senior and executive-level roles are getting impacted the most.  
3. Companies are heavily investing in AI and expecting employees to become “jack of all trades.”  That doesn’t align well with career goals & growth.  
4. Many companies are replacing full-time positions with consultants and contract-based hiring because contractors can be removed anytime without long-term commitments.  
5. The problem is that many of these roles are offering salaries below the candidate’s current market worth.  
6. Your worth should not go backwards. If you are switching jobs, the offer should reflect growth, not a pay cut from your existing base salary.  
7. After 3–4 rounds of interviews, many candidates get rejected in HR discussions simply because companies cannot match their current compensation.  
8. Certifications alone won’t save careers anymore. Real-time experience, execution ability, adaptability, and problem-solving matter far more today.  
9. Keep yourself updated. Learn AI, automation, and modern tools. Also help deserving candidates by sharing genuine knowledge and training.

This market is testing patience, confidence, and adaptability more than technical skills alone.

The people who continuously learn, build, adapt, and refuse to undervalue themselves will eventually come out stronger.

The doors to law just swung wide open in India.⚖️

The Bar Council of India has withdrawn its moratorium on new law colleges — and the ripple effects are already being felt.

And if you're a high schooler even remotely curious about a career in law, this changes your timeline.

Think about what's actually shifting:

→ More law colleges = more seats = less cutthroat competition to get in.
→ A surge in law graduates = a surge in internship demand from firms needing fresh talent.
→ New colleges need to prove themselves = they will chase students, not the other way around.

Now, you might be thinking — "Law is boring. That's not for me."

Fair point. Except law in 2026 looks nothing like your dad's courtroom drama. AI regulation, startup contracts, gaming laws, crypto compliance — every hot industry right now is desperately hiring people who understand both tech and the law.

That's a gap. And gaps are opportunities.

The BCI just created more entry points into one of India's most stable and high-paying professions. The question isn't whether law is a good career. The question is whether you'll research it before everyone else does.

Because by the time it becomes "obvious," the seats will already be taken.

💬 Drop a comment: Are you considering law as a career path? What's stopping you — or what pushed you toward it?

How to Dominate the Sydney Tech Recruitment Market 101… when moving over from the UK

Moving to Sydney is one thing. Owning your market here is another.
Here's what I'd tell every UK tech recruiter landing in Australia:

1. Niche down immediately.
Australia needs 312,000 additional tech workers by 2030, but only produces 7,000 IT graduates a year. 

The gap is enormous. The richest opportunities are in cybersecurity, AI, and cloud:
- 54% of Australian cyber teams are understaffed
- 58% have unfilled positions right now
- 7 of the 10 fastest growing tech roles are AI related

Pick your niche and own it, don’t be a generalist.

2. Speed is your competitive advantage.
The hiring process in the UK taught you rigour, so use what you’ve learnt. 

Sydney's best candidates are off the market in days. Three interview stages max. Fast feedback. No delays. The recruiters winning here are the ones moving the fastest.

3. Build your brand before you land and start posting on LinkedIn before you arrive. 

Sydney is a relationship market. Companies are paying 19% salary premiums and still can't fill roles, but they hire through people they trust. 

Be visible, be credible, be consistent.

4. Don't overlook contracting.
The contractor market in Sydney is mature and lucrative. With companies spending heavily on transformation projects, contract desks are printing right now. If you only focus on perm, you're missing serious opportunity. 

5. Your UK network is an asset, not a liability.
72% of Australian employers actively source talent internationally. 

You're not starting from zero, you're starting with a pipeline.

The market rewards the prepared.

The AI agent just made a $50,000 mistake.

Confident. Convincing. Completely wrong.

So here's the question I keep coming back to:
When an AI agent gets it wrong — who's actually to blame?
1️⃣ The person who built the agent 
2️⃣ The person who used it and trusted the output 
3️⃣ No one — we should accept that AI agents make mistakes
4️⃣ Someone else — tell me in the comments 👇

I have my answer. But I'm genuinely curious where you land — because how we answer this is going to shape every AI policy, SLA contract, and hiring decision for the next decade.

Option 1, 2, 3, or 4? Leave your comments 👇

A founder sent me a line from ChatGPT last week. "Your agency margins should be 40%."

We'd just finished a plan that gets him to 50%+.

The plan is built on an economic model we've been working on together for the last year. It's not the average agency P&L.

Founders are quoting ChatGPT margins back to me as if they were legitimate benchmarks. The number is wrong every time.

The model averages across agencies that haven't done the work. Founders reach for it expecting a stretch number, but they actually get a comfort number.

ChatGPT gave the founder a ceiling, and the founder read it as a target. That's the part that should worry you.

What I'm seeing right now is founders making real decisions against benchmarks the model pulled almost out of thin air.

Hiring decisions. Spend decisions. Growth targets. All sized to a number that thinks smaller than the agency is capable of.

ChatGPT can read your P&L. The economic model behind it lives in your head, your contracts, and what you're building this thing into.

AI is going to keep eating the bottom of the finance stack. The judgement layer is the only part that doesn't average.

A US regional bank just had its CEO's AI clone deliver the prepared remarks on a public earnings call.

Most of the takes will be about the stunt. The actual story is in the contract underneath it.

Customers Bank ($25.9B in assets) signed a multi-year deal with OpenAI to embed engineers inside the bank. AI already writes half their code. They've saved ~28,000 hours — roughly 15 FTEs they didn't have to hire. The CEO is now openly tying AI to revenue-per-employee, not just "productivity."

Three things worth noting if you're a CTO or operator watching this:
1️⃣ The clone is the marketing. The org redesign is the product. Most enterprises will copy the clone and miss the rebuild.
2️⃣ Regulated industries (banking, pharma, process manufacturing) move on this in the same order every time — automate code, then internal ops, then customer-facing. Customers Bank is at step 2. Manufacturing is mostly still at step 1.
3️⃣ "Revenue per employee" is the metric quietly replacing "headcount growth" in board decks. If your 2026 plan still leads with hiring, it's already an old plan.

The interesting question isn't whether your CEO should clone themselves. It's whether your operating model is built for a world where 15% of your work gets done by agents you haven't hired yet.

If your startup is trying to solve its growth pains by hiring layers of middle managers, you are actively injecting corporate cancer into your organization.
Scale usually kills speed because founders build pyramids. A middle manager is an analog router. They take founder intent, filter it through their own ego and career anxiety, and pass down a corrupted message. They add exponential latency to your Value Stream.

Apex founders in 2026 do not build pyramids; they build Distributed Grids. They deploy the Fractal Leadership Model. Here is the exact architectural blueprint for infinite scale:

🛑 1. The Physics of the Pod: Do not divide your company by departments. Build 6-person, cross-functional, autonomous "Pods." Every Pod contains a developer, a legal node, sales, and data. They are sovereign micro-startups. 

⚙️ 2. Programmable Protocols: Replace biological bosses with AI algorithms. Strategy is code. If a Pod hits a verified performance metric, a smart contract automatically unlocks their budget expansion. Zero human approval required. 

🧠 3. The Cell Division Strategy: The human brain cannot manage large groups effectively. When a Pod grows to 8 people, do not hire a manager. The Pod undergoes "Cell Division," splitting into two 4-person parallel Pods that inherit the exact same operational DNA. 

🏗️ 4. The Sovereign Architect: A CEO's job is not to micro-manage humans; it is to eradicate friction in the environment. Deploy Lean Six Sigma to automate compliance, and let the high-velocity Pods execute freely within the guardrails.

Stop hiring analog routers to solve digital scaling problems.
Will you build a bloated, slow bureaucracy, or will you architect an anti-fragile fractal network?

Is your AI tool supporting recruitment or deciding who to hire? 

The ICO's Recruitment Rewired report suggests it's probably making the decisions. Most employers the ICO engaged with get this wrong.

If the AI tool decides who to hire without "meaningful human involvement" – for example, you approve the tool's decision with a click – this is automated decision-making (ADM) for the purposes of the UK GDPR. As a result, the employer has some meaty compliance tasks to complete to make this lawful.

Three things to know:
1) You could use ADM in recruitment.
2) You can't rely on consent or contract for it.
3) You need to satisfy yourself that the AI provider is responsible.

You can read the rest in the blog and tell me, if you have used such tools, are they useful, do you like them; as a candidate, do you trust them?

And if you sell the AI tool, what questions do you get?
